retrieved_chunks.label: Pairwise Correlations
retrieved_chunks.text: Pairwise Correlations: Pairwise correlations are the correlation coefficients between every pair of assets in the portfolio, ranging from −1 (perfectly opposite) to +1 (perfectly in sync). Correlation of 0 means the assets are independent — holding both gives maximum diversification benefit. Correlation above 0.70 means the assets behave almost identically — holding both offers limited risk reduction. The correlation matrix is the normalised version of the covariance matrix: correlation_ij = Σ_ij / (vol_i × vol_j). Critically, correlations are not stable — they tend to spike toward 1.0 during market crises. Example: AAPL and MSFT have a correlation of 0.85, showing near-identical behaviour. Holding 50 percent each is almost the same as holding 100 percent in either — concentration risk remains. AAPL and TLT correlation −0.15: slightly negative — TLT provides mild diversification benefit against equity drawdowns. Risk relevance: Review pairwise correlations to identify clusters of highly correlated assets. A portfolio with all pairs above 0.6 has essentially one concentrated bet regardless of how many tickers it holds. The correlation breakdown in crises is why static allocation is insufficient — dynamic monitoring is needed. Related concepts: covariance_matrix, portfolio_volatility, concentration_risk, diversification.

The portfolio holds 5 positions: AAPL, MSFT, NVDA, SPY, TLT. Annualised return is 18.04 percent, annualised volatility 18.24 percent (high risk). 21-day rolling volatility is 17.7 percent. The portfolio has a Sharpe ratio of 0.72, and a Sortino ratio of1.07 (risk-free rate is 5 percent). Maximum drawdown is -15.69 percent. Beta vs S&P 500 is 0.87.

retrieved_chunks.label: Diversification
retrieved_chunks.text: Diversification: Diversification is the practice of spreading investments across assets with low correlations to reduce portfolio volatility without proportionally reducing expected return. True diversification requires low or negative pairwise correlations — it is not just about holding many tickers. A 20-stock portfolio of US technology stocks is not well-diversified. Effective diversification spans: asset class (equities, bonds, commodities), geography, sector, and factor (growth vs value, cyclical vs defensive). Example: 2022: SPY −18 percent, short-term bonds (SHY) −3.5 percent, commodities (DJP) +20 percent. A 60 percent equity / 30 percent bond / 10 percent commodity portfolio would have lost approximately −8 percent — far less than pure equity. Risk relevance: Diversification is the only free lunch in finance (Markowitz). It reduces volatility without proportionally reducing return. It is the primary defence against concentration risk and single-event losses. Related concepts: correlation, pairwise_correlations, concentration_risk, rebalancing, asset_allocation.
